rows.v: Fresh bread, crackers, light grass, subtle floral hops
The most universally drinkable category — a safe order anywhere
The banana/clove notes come from the yeast strain, not added flavoring — totally natural and a hallmark of the style.
Grapefruit, citrus peel, pine, light floral
The natural next step from a Lager — a gentle introduction to hop character
Spicy curry, blue cheese, grilled meats
"West Coast" IPAs are drier and more bitter; "Hazy/New England" IPAs are softer, juicier, and less bitter despite looking similar in ABV.
Malt-lovers who want flavor without much bitterness
Coffee, dark chocolate, dark fruit, sometimes smoke
Despite the dark color and rich flavor, most stouts are lower in ABV than people expect — Guinness is only about 4.2%.
Lager, lime juice, hot sauce, Worcestershire, salted rim
A Mexican brunch staple — order it if you'd enjoy a Bloody Mary but with beer instead of vodka.
Lager or wheat beer, lemonade or citrus soda
A great hot-weather, low-alcohol option — common at European beer gardens.
Pale ale (bottom), stout floated on top
The float relies on density differences — a skilled bartender pours slowly over a spoon to keep the layers distinct.
Popular in UK pubs — a good option if you like beer but want something a bit lighter and tarter.
Stout or porter, vanilla bean ice cream
Treat it like an adult root beer float — the roasted malt notes pair surprisingly well with vanilla ice cream.
